structured_choices.data_source: De-identified discharge summaries, progress notes, and follow-up messages from 2–3 large academic health systems spanning 3–5 years. Minimum 10,000 clinical notes; stratified sample of ~1,500–2,000 notes manually annotated by clinician-pharmacist pairs for medication, dosage, and adverse-event entity spans with confidence adjudication protocol.
structured_choices.label_definition: Span-level BIO (Begin-Inside-Outside) labels for three entity types: (1) Medication name, (2) Dosage/administration detail, (3) Adverse event mention. Adverse events are restricted to reported or documented side effects; ruled-out concerns and family history are labeled Outside. Inter-annotator agreement (Cohen's κ ≥ 0.75) required before inclusion; disagreement resolved by senior clinical reviewer. Minority class (adverse-event spans) prevalence tracked and reported.
structured_choices.split_method: Patient-stratified, encounter-level temporal split. All notes from a patient are assigned to exactly one fold to prevent data leakage from longitudinal clinical context. Within each health system: (a) assign 60% of unique patients to train, (b) 20% to validation, (c) 20% to test, stratifying by patient age band, primary diagnosis code (ICD10 roll-up), and document type (discharge vs. progress vs. follow-up) to ensure comparable distributions. Temporal order is preserved within each fold: notes are ordered by encounter date; train uses earlier notes, validation uses mid-range, test uses latest. This avoids both patient leakage and future-information leakage.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: Tokenizer, vocabulary, and clinical word embeddings (if used) are fit on the training set only. For the transformer baseline, the pretrained clinical BERT (e.g., SciBERT or BioBERT) is used off-the-shelf; fine-tuning parameters are set via validation set performance. For the CRF baseline, feature scaling (StandardScaler for numeric features like note length, age) and categorical encoding (one-hot for department, medication class) are fit on the training set and applied to validation and test. No label information is used during fitting.
structured_choices.features: Transformer baseline: raw token sequences from full clinical notes (discharge summaries + progress notes from the same encounter window, up to 512 subword tokens). Contextual embeddings come from the pretrained model. CRF baseline: hand-engineered lexical and section features including (1) token surface form, (2) character n-grams (2–4 grams), (3) POS tag (via spacy), (4) section header (regex-matched: 'Medications', 'Assessment', 'Side Effects', etc.), (5) medication dictionary membership (flagged against RxNorm lexicon), (6) note metadata: document type, department, patient age band. Both baselines receive identical token-level context windows (±3 tokens). No features encode the label.
structured_choices.model: Transformer baseline: Clinical BERT (BioBERT or SciBERT) fine-tuned for token classification. Architecture: pretrained encoder + token-level linear head. Hyperparameters: learning rate tuned on validation set (1e-5, 2e-5, 5e-5), batch size (8, 16), max epochs (10–20 with early stopping on validation F1). CRF baseline: scikit-learn or pytorch-crf with L2 regularization, tuned on validation set. Both models trained on identical train set; model selection performed on validation set.
structured_choices.baseline: CRF with hand-engineered features is the baseline. Justification: (1) CRFs are the standard sequence-labeling model in prior clinical NLP work and provide a strong, interpretable comparison; (2) hand-engineered features represent clinically understood signals (section headers, medication dictionaries, POS tags); (3) equal tuning effort—both models have hyperparameters tuned via grid search or Bayesian optimization on the validation set, and both are allowed equivalent computational budget (wall-clock time for tuning). The baseline is not 'rule-based extraction' or 'bag-of-words', both of which would be unfairly weak. This ensures the test isolates the value of transformer architecture and transfer learning, not data volume or feature engineering quality.
structured_choices.primary_metric: Span-level F1 (macro-averaged across the three entity types: Medication, Dosage, Adverse Event). Justification: (1) The clinical use case prioritizes both precision (reducing false positives in safety review) and recall (finding all adverse events); macro-average prevents the frequent classes (Medication, Dosage) from dominating, ensuring adverse-event detection is weighted equally; (2) span-level (not token-level) evaluation matches the downstream task—clinicians extract and review full entity mentions, not individual tokens; (3) F1 is standard in NER literature, enabling comparison to published work. Threshold: entity predictions are compared exactly (span boundaries must match and label must match); no partial credit.
secondary_metrics: Adverse-Event F1 (entity-type-specific): isolates performance on the lowest-prevalence, highest-cost-of-miss class.
Precision and Recall (macro and per-entity-type): reveals whether either model trades off false positives for false negatives.

Calibration: predicted confidence scores are evaluated via calibration plots and expected calibration error (ECE) to assess whether either model's confidence aligns with accuracy; important for clinician trust.
Performance by note type (discharge vs. progress vs. follow-up) and department: checks whether gains generalize or are department/document-specific.
structured_choices.model_selection_data: Validation set (20% of patient-stratified notes) is used exclusively for hyperparameter tuning and model selection. Grid search (or Bayesian optimization) is performed on validation F1 for both the transformer and CRF. Early stopping during training (if applicable) is based on validation F1. The test set is held out and touched only once, at the very end, to produce the final performance numbers.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: The test set (20% of patient-stratified, temporally held-out notes) is evaluated exactly once after all model selection is complete. No iterative refinement based on test results. Performance is reported with 95% confidence intervals (via bootstrap or stratified sampling) and broken down by entity type, note type, and department to characterize the scope of the comparison.
structured_choices.confound_controls: To address the known confound that transformer advantage may stem from richer context rather than sequence modeling: (1) Both models receive identical input—the full token sequence from each note, with the same preprocessing and tokenization. (2) The CRF baseline is given access to section headers and clinical metadata (note type, department, patient age), which are structured signals that a critic might claim the transformer exploits; this levels the playing field. (3) Ablation: report performance of a 'CRF + fine-tuned embeddings' variant that uses pretrained clinical word embeddings (Word2Vec or FastText trained on clinical text) instead of hand-engineered features, to separate the contribution of pretraining from the transformer architecture itself. (4) Cross-system generalization: evaluate both models on notes from a held-out health system (if data from 3+ systems is available) to test whether gains are system-specific or robust. (5) Document length stratification: report performance separately for short vs. long notes to ensure the transformer's advantage (or disadvantage) is not driven by length bias.

design_narrative:
## Experiment Design: Transformer vs. CRF for Clinical Entity Extraction

### 1. Data Collection and Annotation

We would assemble de-identified clinical notes (discharge summaries, progress notes, follow-up messages) spanning 3–5 years from 2–3 large academic health systems, totaling at least 10,000 notes. From this corpus, we would randomly sample and stratify approximately 1,500–2,000 notes for manual annotation by clinician-pharmacist pairs.

Annotation targets three entity types: Medication names, Dosage/administration details, and Adverse events. Adverse events are restricted to documented or reported side effects; ruled-out concerns and family history are labeled Outside. Disagreements are resolved by senior clinical review; all included annotations must achieve Cohen's κ ≥ 0.75. We track and report the class imbalance—particularly the prevalence of adverse-event spans, which are likely the rarest and highest-value class.

### 2. Train/Validation/Test Split

The split must prevent patient-level leakage while respecting temporal structure. We assign each patient to exactly one fold, then allocate 60% of unique patients to training, 20% to validation, and 20% to test. Within each health system, we stratify by patient age band, primary diagnosis (ICD10), and document type (discharge vs. progress vs. follow-up) to ensure comparable distributions across splits.

Within each fold, we order notes by encounter date. The training set uses earlier notes, validation uses mid-range encounters, and the test set uses the latest notes. This preserves temporal validity—the model never trains on future information—and reflects real-world deployment, where new patients and future notes are evaluated without access to their own longitudinal history.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

**Transformer baseline:** We use a pretrained clinical BERT model (BioBERT or SciBERT). Input is the raw token sequence from each clinical note, up to 512 subword tokens. Contextual embeddings come directly from the pretrained model; fine-tuning is performed on the training set.

**CRF baseline:** We construct hand-engineered lexical and section features: (1) token surface form, (2) character n-grams (2–4 grams), (3) POS tag via spaCy, (4) section header (regex-matched: 'Medications', 'Assessment', 'Adverse Reactions', etc.), (5) membership in RxNorm medication dictionary, and (6) note metadata (document type, department, patient age band). To ensure fairness, the CRF receives identical token-level context windows (±3 tokens).

All preprocessing transformers (StandardScaler, one-hot encoders) are fit on the training set only and applied to validation and test. No label information is used during fitting.

### 4. Model Training and Hyperparameter Tuning

**Transformer:** Fine-tune the pretrained model end-to-end on the training set. Hyperparameter grid: learning rate ∈ {1e-5, 2e-5, 5e-5}, batch size ∈ {8, 16}, max epochs ∈ {10, 20} with early stopping on validation F1. We tune on the validation set via grid search or Bayesian optimization.

**CRF:** Train with L2 regularization on the training set. Tune regularization strength and any chain hyperparameters on the validation set via grid search. Tuning effort is equivalent: both models receive the same computational budget.

### 5. Model Selection and Validation

The validation set is used exclusively for hyperparameter selection. For each candidate model, we evaluate span-level F1 (macro-averaged across entity types) on the validation set. The model with the highest validation F1 is selected. The test set is held out and never touched during tuning.

### 6. Evaluation and Primary Metric

The primary metric is **span-level F1, macro-averaged across Medication, Dosage, and Adverse Event.** We justify this choice: (1) Span-level matching reflects the clinical workflow—clinicians extract and review full entity mentions, not individual tokens. (2) Macro-averaging prevents frequent classes (Medication and Dosage) from dominating the score; adverse-event detection, though rare, is weighted equally and reflects the clinical priority. (3) F1 balances precision and recall, both essential in safety pharmacovigilance. (4) Span boundaries must match exactly; no partial credit.

### 7. Secondary Metrics and Subgroup Analysis

We report:
- **Adverse-Event F1** (entity-type-specific) to isolate performance on the highest-cost-of-miss class.
- **Precision and Recall** (macro and per-type) to detect precision-recall tradeoffs.
- **Calibration** (ECE, calibration plots) to assess whether either model's confidence aligns with accuracy.
- **Performance by document type** (discharge, progress, follow-up) and **by department** to characterize generalization.

### 8. Confound Controls and Robustness

To rule out confounds:
- Both models receive identical token-level input and preprocessing.
- The CRF baseline is given structured metadata (section headers, note type, department) to level the playing field against the transformer's access to rich context.
- **Ablation:** We train a CRF + fine-tuned clinical embeddings variant to isolate the value of sequence modeling from pretraining.
- **Cross-system generalization (if available):** Evaluate both models on notes from a held-out health system to test robustness.
- **Document length stratification:** Report performance separately for short vs. long notes to ensure the transformer is not simply exploiting length.

### 9. Final Test Evaluation and Reporting

After model selection is complete, we evaluate both the transformer and CRF on the held-out test set exactly once. We report the primary metric (macro F1) with 95% confidence intervals (via bootstrap), broken down by entity type, document type, and department. We also report the secondary metrics and ablation results to contextualize the claim: the comparison isolates the value of the transformer architecture and transfer learning, given equal tuning effort and comparable feature sets.
